nativeRange.habitat: In the wild, Rhinogobius gobies inhabit fast-flowing, well-oxygenated mountain streams and rivers with rocky or gravelly substrates, typically found in riffles and runs where the current is strong and the water is clear and cool.

decorPreferences: smooth river stones and rounded pebbles for territorial markers and resting
flat slate or rock pieces forming caves and overhangs
coarse sand or fine gravel substrate
minimal planting — hardy low-light plants (Java fern, Anubias) attached to rocks
powerhead or canister filter outlet directed to create visible surface current and high dissolved oxygen

diet.notes: Micro-carnivore in the wild, feeding on small invertebrates, insect larvae, amphipods, and worms. In the aquarium, readily accepts high-quality frozen and live foods: bloodworm, white worm, Daphnia, Moina, brine shrimp, and tubifex. Sinking pellets and micro-wafers accepted by well-acclimated individuals. Live or frozen foods should form the majority of the diet. Avoid dry-food-only feeding; the species may decline without regular meaty supplements.

goodWith: small, fast-moving danios and rasboras that occupy upper water column
small cool-water catfish (e.g., Corydoras sterbai in cooler setups)
peaceful nano fish that do not compete for bottom territory
avoidWith: other Rhinogobius males (intense territorial disputes; one-male-per-tank rule applies)
other bottom-dwelling gobies or loaches that encroach on territory
slow-moving or long-finned fish susceptible to nipping
tropical fish requiring temperatures above 26°C (thermal incompatibility)
large aggressive cichlids or predatory fish

imageCandidates.notes: Rhinogobius flumineus by Seotaro, 3008x2000. Tack-sharp in-situ shot on a mossy stream boulder; whole fish, raised dorsal, vivid golden-yellow reticulated flank pattern and blue-white belly speckling. Best representative of the genus.
Rhinogobius fluviatilis by Totti, 2894x1368. Sharp side profile over natural gravel; whole fish with red facial striping, blue-speckled flanks and raised dorsal. Distinct species.

Rhinogobius giurinus by Σ64, 5100x3400. Crisp pair (two individuals) over sand and driftwood, showing reticulated head markings and blue flank speckles; good group representation for a distinct species.

Labeled Rhinogobius zhoui male (Andrew Bogott), 3444x1491. Sharp whole-fish profile with orange chevron flank bars and red-striped dorsal/anal fins; colouration atypical for classic R. zhoui so treat ID at genus level. Adds a distinct colour form.
Rhinogobius duospilus by Neale Monks, 320x240 (low resolution). Two individuals over sand; duospilus is the flagship trade species of the genus, included for species diversity despite small size.

summary: The Rhinogobius Goby is a large East Asian genus of true freshwater stream gobies — with over 100 described species — prized for vivid male coloration and active bottom-dwelling behavior. These cool-water fish (18–24°C) need strong current and high oxygen; males tend to be intensely territorial, so only one per tank is generally advised.
careNotes: Rhinogobius is a species complex of over 100 described species distributed across China, Japan, South Korea, and Taiwan, with many trade specimens sold without species-level identification. Most commonly encountered in the hobby are R. duospilus, R. wui, R. zhoui, and various undescribed forms from southern China and Taiwan. All share broadly similar requirements: cool, fast-moving, highly oxygenated water is essential — a powerhead or strong canister filter return that creates visible surface agitation and ripple is strongly recommended. Temperatures above 26°C are stressful and eventually lethal; 18–22°C is the preferred range for most species. A tank of at least 60 L with a 60 cm footprint gives one male adequate territory; a 90–120 cm tank allows a breeding pair or one male with two females if multiple hides are provided. Substrate of smooth rounded pebbles and coarse sand replicates natural riverbed conditions and allows males to establish and defend territories among rocks. Males tend to be intensely territorial toward conspecific males and will fight persistently — only one male should be kept per tank. A pair (1 male + 1–2 females) is the standard approach; females are generally more tolerant of one another. Males display vivid red, orange, or blue facial and flank markings during courtship; females are cryptically colored. Water quality should be kept excellent: Rhinogobius are sensitive to accumulated nitrates and low oxygen, and dissolved oxygen saturation should be maintained through vigorous surface agitation. Regular partial water changes (30–40% weekly) are strongly recommended. The genus is true freshwater and should never be exposed to salt. Acclimation to captivity takes time — offer live or frozen micro-foods initially.
breedingNotes: Rhinogobius species are cave-spawners. A conditioned pair will spawn in a rock cave or under a flat stone, with the male guarding the clutch of adhesive eggs. The male fans and guards the eggs until hatching (approximately 5–10 days at 20–22°C). Fry are very small and require infusoria or micro-rotifers initially, graduating to baby brine shrimp nauplii within the first week. Breeding has been achieved in captivity and is considered moderately difficult, requiring a cool, high-flow dedicated breeding setup, a well-conditioned pair, and provision of appropriate spawning sites (flat-topped stones or PVC pipe caves). Raising fry successfully is the most challenging aspect due to their small initial size.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Rhinogobius sp.
primary.notes: Primary source for water parameters, tank requirements, territorial behavior, diet, and breeding notes. Confirms cool-water preference (18–24°C), high-flow requirement, one-male-per-tank rule, cave-spawning behavior, and carnivorous micro-invertebrate diet.

additional.name: FishBase — Rhinogobius duospilus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to ~6 cm), native range (southern China, Hong Kong, possibly introduced Taiwan), taxonomy (Oxudercidae, Gobiiformes), habitat (fast-flowing streams, gravel/rocky substrates), and diet (small benthic invertebrates). Confirms freshwater-only status.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Goby Care
additional.notes: General freshwater goby husbandry guide. Confirms carnivore diet (live/frozen micro-foods preferred), bottom-dwelling behavior, territorial nature toward conspecifics, and high-oxygen water requirement for stream gobies.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Rhinogobius species guide
additional.notes: Confirms species complex with 100+ described species, East Asian distribution (China, Japan, Korea, Taiwan), cool-water requirement (18–24°C), strong current preference, one-male territorial rule, and vivid male coloration as distinguishing feature. Notes importation under multiple trade names.

additional.name: FishBase — Rhinogobius genus summary
additional.notes: Taxonomic confirmation of placement in Oxudercidae (formerly Gobiidae subfamily), order Gobiiformes. Genus contains 100+ valid species. Supports freshwater-only classification for the genus.

fish.breedingNotes: Rhinogobius gobies are cave-spawners: the male guards an adhesive egg clutch on the underside of a flat rock or inside a cave. Fry are very small and require infusoria initially, graduating to baby brine shrimp nauplii within the first week. Breeding has been achieved in captivity using a dedicated cool-water, high-flow setup with a well-conditioned pair and suitable spawning sites.
